sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 3, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - A black maze structure with white passages and red paths - Blue regions appear to be player/agent positions (one inside the maze at rows 8-12, one at bottom-right) - Red (R) forms a path running down the right wall and across the bottom - Top-left blue square with white center may be a legend/indicator - Goal likely involves navigating through the maze, possibly following the red path
- Goal model: Navigate the blue player through the maze, likely following the red path to the goal
- Action model: UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT move the player; SPACE purpose unknown
- Plan: First probe with a single move to understand player movement, then solve the maze
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 2 of about 78 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The only change was:
1. Node 13 (blue bar at bottom row 63) lost one pixel (64 -> 63) - this is the HUD/timer bar shrinking
2. A new node 14 appeared with 1 light gray pixel
So DOWN didn't move the player at all - it just shrunk the timer bar by 1 pixel. This is a wasted move! The player can't move DOWN from the current position because there's a wall.
Let me reconsider. The player moved RIGHT successfully (from cols 14-18 to 20-24), but DOWN didn't work. This means the player can only move in directions where there's an opening.
Looking at the maze:
- Player is at rows 8-12, cols 20-24
- Below (row 13) is all black wall
- So DOWN is blocked
Let me check what directions are open. The player is in a room at rows 8-12, cols 20-24. Looking at the ASCII:
- Row 8: BBBBBBBbbbbbBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBWWWWWW
- Row 9: BBBBBBBbbbbbBBBBBBBBBBBBBBRRRBBWWWWWW
- Row 10: BBBBBBBbbBbbBBBBBBBBBBBBBBRRRBBWWWWWW
- Row 11: BBBBBBBbbbbbBBBBBBBBBBBBBBRRRBBWWWWWW
- Row 12: BBBBBBBbbbbbBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBRBBBWWWWWW
The player is surrounded by black walls on all sides in this room. But RIGHT worked! So the movement must be teleporting to the next room in the direction pressed.
Wait - the player started at cols 14-18 and moved to cols 20-24 with RIGHT. But looking at the initial state, the player was at cols 14-18 which was the blue area. After RIGHT, it moved to cols 20-24. But the ASCII still shows the player at the left side.
Actually, I think I misread the initial position. Let me re-check. The initial player boundary was [[8, 14], [8, 18], [12, 18], [12, 14]] which means rows 8-12, cols 14-18. After RIGHT, it became [[8, 20], [8, 24], [12, 24], [12, 20]] which is rows 8-12, cols 20-24.
So the player moved 6 columns to the right. But looking at the ASCII, the player is still at the left side of the maze. This is confusing.
Wait, let me re-read the ASCII more carefully:
Row 8: BBBBBBBbbbbbBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBWWWWWW
This is cols 13-49 of the full grid. So:
- Cols 13-19: BBBBBBB (7 B's)
- Cols 20-24: bbbbb (5 b's - player)
- Cols 25-45: BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBB (21 B's)
- Cols 46-49: WWWWWW (6 W's)
So the player IS at cols 20-24. The initial position was cols 14-18, which would have been:
Row 8: BBBBBBbbbbbBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBWWWWWW
- Cols 13-18: BBBBBB (6 B's)
- Cols 19-23: bbbbb (5 b's)
Wait, that doesn't match either. Let me just accept that the player moved from cols 14-18 to cols 20-24.
The key insight is: the player teleports between rooms. RIGHT moved the player to the next room to the right. DOWN didn't work because there's no room below.
Now I need to figure out where the player can go from the current position. The player is at rows 8-12, cols 20-24. Looking at the maze:
- To the right: more black walls, then the red path area
- Below: black walls at row 13, then white passages at rows 14-18
- To the left: the original blue room
Since DOWN didn't work, maybe the player needs to follow the red path? Or maybe the player moves along corridors?
Let me try RIGHT again to see if the player moves further right.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model (updated):
- DOWN was a wasted move - only the timer bar (node 13) shrunk by 1 pixel
- Player didn't move because there's a wall below
- RIGHT successfully moved the player 6 columns right
- Player movement is teleportation between rooms, not continuous

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try RIGHT again to see if player moves further
result = action(['RIGHT'])
print(f"After RIGHT: board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['id'] == 6:
        print(f"Player: boundary={n['boundary']}")
    if n['id'] == 8:
        print(f"Black dot: boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Show the area around the player
ascii_grid = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("\nMaze around player (rows 7-15, cols 13-50):")
for i in range(7, 16):
    row = ascii_grid[i]
    print(f"Row {i:2d}: {row[13:50]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

Black dot: boundary=[[10, 28]]

Row  8: BBBBBBBBBBBBBbbbbbBBBBBBBBBBBBBWWWWWW
Row  9: BBBBBBBBBBBBBbbbbbBBBBBBBBRRRBBWWWWWW
Row 10: BBBBBBBBBBBBBbbBbbBBBBBBBBRRRBBWWWWWW
Row 11: BBBBBBBBBBBBBbbbbbBBBBBBBBRRRBBWWWWWW
Row 12: BBBBBBBBBBBBBbbbbbBBBBBBBBBRBBBWWWWWW
Row 13: BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBRBBBWWWWWW
Row 14: BBBBBBBWWWWWBBBBBBBWWWWWWWBRBWWWWWWWW
Row 15: BBBBBBBWWWWWBBBBBBBWWWWWWWBRBWWWWWWWW
